content.text: The Executor's Role
In a Planner-Executor architecture, the
Executor

receives a validated plan and carries it out step by step. The Executor's job is purely mechanical: dispatch each step's tool call, record the result, and make it available to subsequent steps that depend on it. The Executor does not reason about the goal or decide what to do next — those decisions were made by the Planner.
The Executor has three core responsibilities:
Dependency ordering
: execute steps in the order their dependencies allow. Steps with no dependencies can run first. Steps that depend on others must wait for their predecessors to complete.
Result recording
: store each step's output so that later steps can reference it.
Failure handling
: decide what to do when a step fails — retry, skip, abort the whole plan, or trigger the Planner to replan.
Sequential Executor
The baseline Executor processes steps one at a time in dependency order. This is the simplest implementation and is correct for all plans. Parallelism can be added later as an optimization.
The
_execution_order
method produces a valid execution sequence that respects all
depends_on
declarations. Within a wave of steps that are all ready to execute, they are sorted by
step_id
for deterministic ordering. The
_results
dictionary accumulates results so that later steps can access earlier steps' outputs via
get_result(step_id)
Parallel Executor with Execution Waves
When the plan has independent steps (no shared dependencies), the Executor can run them in parallel. The
execution_waves
method groups steps into waves: all steps in the same wave can start at the same time. Using
ThreadPoolExecutor
executes each wave's steps concurrently.

The parallel executor runs steps in the same wave concurrently. Wall-clock time for a plan with N independent steps shrinks from N * (step_time) to approximately 1 * (step_time) + overhead. Thread pool overhead is small for I/O-bound tool calls (API requests, database reads). For CPU-bound operations, use
ProcessPoolExecutor
instead.
Step Failure Strategies
When a step fails, the Executor must decide what to do with the steps that depend on it. The right strategy depends on whether the downstream steps
require
the failed step's result:
Failure strategy
What happens to downstream steps
When to use
All downstream steps are skipped; execution stops
When any failure means the goal cannot be achieved
Only steps that directly or transitively depend on the failed step are skipped; others continue
When the plan has independent sub-goals; partial results are useful
The failed step is retried 1-3 times before being marked failed
When failures are transient (network error, rate limit)
Executor returns 'partial' status; host code sends results + failure reason back to Planner
When an alternative path may achieve the goal
The Executor's job is to dispatch plan steps in dependency order, record results, and handle failures.

Use sequential execution as the baseline; add parallel execution when the plan has independent steps in the same wave. Choose the failure strategy based on whether the goal requires all steps to succeed (abort) or can tolerate partial success (skip). Retry transient failures; trigger replanning for environment-change failures.
